coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible liquid-type carbon nanodots (C-paints) as light delivery materials for cell growth and astaxanthin induction of Haematococcus pluvialis

coredata.dc:description: In this study, we aimed to demonstrate the feasibility of the application of biocompatible liquid type fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-paints) to microalgae by improving microalgae productivity. C-paints were prepared by a simple process of ultrasound irradiation using polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a passivation agent. The resulting C-paints exhibited a carbonyl-rich surface with good uniformity of particle size, excellent water solubility, photo-stability, fluorescence efficiency, and good biocompatibility (<10.0 mg mL− 1 of C-paints concentration). In the practical application of C-paints to microalgae culture, the most effective and optimized condition leading to growth promoting effect was observed at a C-paints concentration of 1.0 mg mL− 1 (>20% higher than the control cell content). A C-paints concentration of 1–10.0 mg mL− 1 induced an approximately >1.8 times higher astaxanthin content than the control cells. The high light delivery effect of non-cytotoxic C-paints was applied as a stress condition for H. pluvialis growth and was found to play a major role in enhancing productivity. Notably, the results from this study are an essential approach to improve astaxanthin production, which can be used in various applications because of its therapeutic effects such as cancer prevention, anti-inflammation, immune stimulation, and treatment of muscle-soreness.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we aimed to demonstrate the feasibility of the application of biocompatible liquid type fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-paints) to microalgae by improving microalgae productivity. C-paints were prepared by a simple process of ultrasound irradiation using polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a passivation agent. The resulting C-paints exhibited a carbonyl-rich surface with good uniformity of particle size, excellent water solubility, photo-stability, fluorescence efficiency, and good biocompatibility (<10.0 mg mL− 1 of C-paints concentration). In the practical application of C-paints to microalgae culture, the most effective and optimized condition leading to growth promoting effect was observed at a C-paints concentration of 1.0 mg mL− 1 (>20% higher than the control cell content). A C-paints concentration of 1–10.0 mg mL− 1 induced an approximately >1.8 times higher astaxanthin content than the control cells. The high light delivery effect of non-cytotoxic C-paints was applied as a stress condition for H. pluvialis growth and was found to play a major role in enhancing productivity. Notably, the results from this study are an essential approach to improve astaxanthin production, which can be used in various applications because of its therapeutic effects such as cancer prevention, anti-inflammation, immune stimulation, and treatment of muscle-soreness.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNT), carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene, graphite, and fullerene have widely been studied because of their advantages such as great mechanical strength, large theoretical specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, high transparency, and high flexibility [1–4]. Among these materials, CNDs have received considerable attention as promising candidates for sensing, as catalysts, as optoelectronics, and for biotechnological applications [4–7] because of their remarkable biocompatibility; good optical properties; low toxicity; easy surface functionalization; and high water solubility. Furthermore, CNDs can typically be produced in both top-down and bottom-up routes. In the top-down route, bulk materials are decomposed to synthesize CNDs [8]; some of these methods include electrochemical exfoliation [9], laser ablation [10], and plasma treatment [11]. In contrast, the bottom-up route, in which CNDs are synthesized from the raw material, includes combustion [12], hydrothermal treatment [13], microwave treatment [14], ultrasonic treatment [15], and passivation [4,6,7,16]. Surface passivation using functionalization precursors enhances the fluorescence of CNDs in the visible and near infrared spectral regions [3,17–20]. Polyethylene glycol (PEG), a biocompatible, non-conjugated polymer, is a well-known passivation agent that can readily be applied to CNDs. For example, Sun et al. improved the fluorescence yield of CNDs using passivation agent PEG1500N [18]. However, the process of synthesizing PEG passivated CNDs involves complicated steps with high temperatures (120–230 °C) and long reaction times (9–72 h) [3,17,19,20]. Consequently, some issues still exist in finding suitable fabrication methods that meet the high efficiency, simplicity, price competitiveness, and mass production of PEGs. In addition, the preparation condition of materials (e.g. environmental friendly design, mild synthesis conditions, and biocompatibility) are important considerations for biological and environmental applications (e.g. nanocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, and cultivation of microalgae) [7].

Over the past decade, microalgae, which are a type of photosynthetic microorganisms, have attracted enormous attention in the academic and industrial sectors for various applications such as pharmaceuticals, nutraceuticals, cosmetics, functional foods, aquaculture, and biofuels [21,22]. The effects of numerous astaxanthins (AXTs; 3,3′-dihydroxy-β-carotene-4,4′-dione) have been evaluated in human clinical trials for cancer prevention and myasthenia gravis treatment, and for their anti-inflammation and immune-boosting effects, for example. Application of natural AXT of Haematococcusis also preferred over chemically synthesized forms in the healthcare market like neutraceutical supplements [23]. Previous studies on cultivation of microalgae have focused on improving productivity by controlling the variety of stress conditions, including nutrient deficiency [24], salinity [25], pH [26], temperature [27], and light intensity [28]. In particular, the abundant light quantity provided is a vital factor that increases the productivity of large-scale cultivation of microalgae. However, the penetration depth of light, in the practical culture of microalgae, is limited to below 5 cm [29]. Therefore, light delivery systems [30,31], such as optical fibers, Fresnel lenses, and temporal light dilution, have been proposed to extend the quantity of light exposed to microalgae, but there are still problems that need to be solved [29,32,33].

Although CNDs are now emerging as promising fluorescent nanomaterials, limited attempts have been made to apply CNDs as the optical medium for the cultivation of microalgae for enhancing growth and metabolite production [34–36]. Xiao et al. evaluated the toxicity of six types of quantum dots (QDs) as fluorescent nanomaterials on microalgae in 2016 [37]. The effect of QDs on Chlorella could be explained by measuring the inhibition of seaweed growth, chlorophyll-a content, protein content, and enzyme and metabolite content and via acute toxicity tests. In addition, Abu-Ghosh et al. showed that nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N@CDs) can enhance microalgal AXT formation by converting the UV light of the solar spectrum to blue light [38]. The N@CDs in this study were synthesized from bovine serum albumin (BSA) at 195 °C for 6 h. However, it should be noted that these materials are expensive, and their biocompatibilities have not been evaluated. In addition, the photostability and dispersibility of N@CDs are still relatively insufficient to apply them to large scale biological processes.

High nanoparticle concentrations in microalgal solutions can hinder the effective transfer of light into microalgal cells. In efforts to avoid this problem, indirect use of metal nanoparticles coupled to localized surface plasmon resonances (LSPR) placed outside of closed photobioreactors has been applied [39,40].

In this study, a simple and eco-friendly approach for large-scale synthesis of liquid-type fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-paints) was explored, which has huge potential for environmental and biotechnological applications. The C-paints passivated with PEG were prepared by ultrasound irradiation under a mild reaction temperature of 60 °C and short time of 2 h. The C-paints were used as a multi-directional light source to increase the dispersion and intensity of light and consequently boost microalgae productivity. The biocompatibility was initially confirmed by cytotoxicity evaluation to apply C-paints to culture of microalgae. In addition, excellent growth promotion and induction efficiency for lipids and AXTs was achieved by the high light delivery effect induced by non-cytotoxic C-paints. The biocompatibility of C-paints can expand its range of applications to improve the productivity of high value-added materials of microalgae, too various autotrophic organisms, and to diverse medical fields.

To produce single-step C-paints, PEG (placed in 100-mL glass bottles) was subjected to ultrasonic irradiation at 60 °C (frequency = 40 kHz) for 2 h. Biocompatible polymer PEG (average Mn = 300, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used as a passivation agent to enhance the fluorescence of C-paints. We performed the entire experiment without additional solvents. Ultimately, brown, liquid-type C-paints including carbon nanodots was obtained. For environmental friendly light delivery materials, we performed the experiments in optimal non-toxic conditions (<5 h; <5 mg mL− 1) to increase the efficiency of C-paints.

Transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) samples were arranged by placing C-paints (30 μL) on a carbon-coated copper grid (300-mesh) and drying the C-paints at room temperature. The morphological structure and size of the C-paints was analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope version 4.31 controller with a multimode AFM, Digital Instrument, USA). The chemical properties of the C-paints were evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and these data were documented using a Kratos Analytical instrument (monochromatic X-ray source with Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV), AXIS Nova, UK) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, FTIR4100/Jasco). UV/Vis absorption spectra were measured by a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 5000, Australia).

The cytotoxicity of the C-paints was examined using an MTT assay. HepG2 (human liver cancer cell line) and A549 (adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cell line) cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 8 × 103 cells per well and cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C for 24, 48, and 72 h under a 5% CO2 atmosphere in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and/or Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)-1640 supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin antibiotics. Subsequently, 20 μL of 0.2 mg mL− 1 MTT solution in medium was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, the optical density (OD) was measured at 490 nm using an absorbance microplate reader (EMax microplate reader, Bucher Biotec AG, Basel, Switzerland).

Two green microalgal species, Chlorella sp. KR-1 and Haematococcus pluvialis, were used in this study. Chlorella sp. KR-1, an oleaginous freshwater microalga, was isolated from a stream in Yeongwol-gun, Korea [41]. Modified N8 medium (constituents: KNO3, 5 mM; KH2PO4, 5.44 mM; Na2HPO4, 1.83 mM; MgSO4·7H2O, 0.20 mM; CaCl2, 0.12 mM; FeNaEDTA, 0.03 mM; ZnSO4·7H2O, 0.01 mM; MnCl2·4H2O, 0.07 mM; CuSO4, 0.07 mM; and Al2(SO4)3·18H2O, 0.01 mM) was used for the photoautotrophic growth of KR-1. Batch cultivations were performed at 25 °C in a gyratory incubator at a shaking speed of 150 rpm (IS-971RF, Lab Companion, Korea). The incubator was maintained under continuous illumination (36 μmol photon m− 2 s− 1) using white fluorescent lamps. An Erlenmeyer flask of 250 mL (working vol., 100 mL) with a porous silicon stopper (Shin-Etsu Chemical Co., Japan) was used. The seed for the main culture was incubated for 14 days after inoculating one colony of KR-1 grown on an agar-plate. The inoculum concentration was adjusted to an initial OD (0.1) at 660 nm, and then, the main cultures were performed for 22 days using the same flasks. The detailed experimental conditions are available elsewhere [42].

Haematococcus pluvialis NIES-144 was secured from National Institute for Environmental Studies (NIES), University of Tokyo, Japan. The NIES medium with the following composition (per liter) was used for vegetative cell growth: Ca(NO3)2, 0.15 g; KNO3, 0.10 g; β-glycerophosphoric acid disodium salt pentahydrate, 0.05 g; MgSO4·7H2O, 0.04 g; Tris-aminomethane, 0.50 g; thiamine, 0.01 mg; PIV metal solution, 3.00 mL; biotin 0.10 μg; and vitamin B12, 0.10 μg. One liter of PIV metal solution comprised Na2EDTA, 1.0 g; FeCl3·6H2O, 0.196 g; MnCl2·4H2O, 36.0 mg; ZnSO4·7H2O, 22.0 mg; CoCl2·6H2O, 4.0 mg; and Na2MoO4·2H2O, 2.5 mg [43]. After adjusting the pH to 7.5, the medium was sterilized by filtration through a 0.2 μm pore membrane (Satorius Stedium Biotech., Germany). One colony of H. pluvialis grown on an agar-plate was transferred to a flask (250 mL) with a porous silicon stopper (working volume, 100 mL). The flask was stirred for 14 days in a shaking incubator under the same culture conditions for KR-1. A vegetative cell culture of H. pluvialis was performed in a 250 mL-scale, funnel-type photobioreactor (working volume, 150 mL). The injected concentration was adapted to an initial OD of 0.03 at 680 nm. The photobioreactor was continuously supplied with 5% (v/v) CO2 in air at a rate of 60 mL min− 1 from the bottom of the reactor, as previously optimized. The gas was supplied through a 0.2-μm PTFE venting filter (Minisart 2000, Satorius Stedium Biotech., Germany) and managed using mass flow controllers (MK Precision, Korea) and flow meters (Kojima Instrument Inc., Japan). The photobioreactor was incubated for 14 days in a plant growth chamber (GC-300, JEIO TECH, Korea) at 25 °C and illuminated at 60 μmol photon m− 2 s− 1 on a 12:12 h dark/light cycle using white fluorescent lamps.

C-paints were added to the microalgal cultures at concentrations of 1.0–50.0 mg mL− 1 for Chlorella sp. KR-1 and 1.0–10.0 mg mL− 1 for H. pluvialis using sterile hypodermic disposable syringes. Regardless of their concentrations, the carbon nanodot solution did not exhibit a considerable OD difference at 660–680 nm (UV–vis spectrophotometer, Optizen 2120UV, Mecasys Co., Korea) (data not shown).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total lipid and AXT content estimations
ce:section.ce:para: The total lipids of H. pluvialis (freeze-dried cyst cells) were extracted using a chloroform/methanol mixture, and their content was predicted using fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) via a direct transesterification method [41]. Chloroform/methanol (2:1, v/v) (2 mL) was added to the cells (ca. 10 mg) in a 12-mL glass tube and mixed forcefully for 10 min (Voltex 3, IKA, Germany) at ambient temperature. Chloroform (1 mL) containing heptadecanoic acid (C17:0) as an internal standard (500 μg L− 1), methanol (1 mL), and H2SO4 (95%) (300 μL) were added to the tube and vortex-mixed for 5 min (Voltex 3, IKA, Germany). After incubation at 100 °C using a heating-block reactor for 10 min (HS R200, Humas, Korea), the tube was cooled to room temperature. Distilled water (1 mL) was added and combined for 5 min. The lipid-containing organic phase was filtered by a 0.2 μm syringe filter (Sartorius, Germany) after centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min (Combi-514R, Hanil Science Inc., Korea), and analyzed by GC.

The total AXT content of H. pluvialis (freeze-dried cells) cyst biomass was determined by glass-bead-grinding acetone extraction. Acetone (1 mL) and glass beads (1.0 g, 1 mm diameter, Daihan Scientific, Korea) were added to the cells (ca. 16 mg) in a tube (2 mL) and then blended forcefully for 10 min (Voltex 3, IKA, Germany) under room temperature. The AXT-containing acetone layer was accumulated in a 12-mL amber glass tube after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 1 min (Smart 13, Hanil Science Inc., Korea). Acetone extraction was replicated five times until all the red-colored cyst cells became colorless. The AXT-containing acetone layer was evaporated using an N-EVAP evaporator (40 °C, Organomation Associates Inc., USA). The AXT extract was diluted with 0.025 N NaOH solutions (1 mL), stirred at 4 °C for 12 h (under darkness condition), and analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).
Using FAME, AXT contents in H. pluvialis per cell were measured by the equation:

FAME (mg FAME cell− 1) = (FAME content × DCW) / Cell number × 1000 (1) Astaxanthin (mg AXT cell− 1) = (AXT content × DCW) / Cell number (2) (FAME content: mg FAME g cell− 1, DCW: g cell L·media− 1, Cell number: ea. mL·media− 1, AXT content: mg AXT mg cell− 1).

The absorption spectrum of C-paints was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometry (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded using a UV trans-illuminator (DUT-260; Core Bio System, Korea) to measure the optical properties of C-paints. The excitation wavelengths were 290–550 nm. The time-course profiles of the microalgal growth were measured at ODs of 660 and 680 nm for Chlorella sp. KR-1 and H. pluvialis, respectively (UV–vis spectrophotometer, Optizen 2120UV, Mecasys Co., Korea). The final biomass concentrations after 22 days (for Chlorella) and 14 days (for Haematococcus) were estimated as dry cell weight (DCW). Fresh samples were filtered through pre-weighed GF/C filters (Whatman, UK), double washed with distilled water, dried at 70 °C for 24 h, and weighted. The pH values and light intensities were measured with a pH meter (DKK-TOA Co., Japan) and a quantum meter (LI-250A, LI-COR Inc., USA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy for ultrastructure of algae
ce:section.ce:para: Control and C-paints treated H. pluvialis were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde, stained with 1% osmium tetroxide, washed with PBS, dehydrated in ethanol and propylene oxide, embedded in Epon 812, and polymerized. Sections were counterstained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate and visualized with a Bio-HVEM system (JEM-1400 Plus at 120 kV and JEM-1000 BEF at 1000 kV; JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan).

H. pluvialis (freeze-dried 100 mg cyst cells) was combined with a distilled water (2 mL) mixture in a 6-mL glass tube. The tube was further stirred at room temperature after addition of hexane (2 mL) for 6 h at 500 rpm. The lipid-containing hexane layer was divided (4000 rpm, 10 min; Combi-514R, Hanil Science Inc., Korea), collected, and evaporated using a nitrogen evaporator (40 °C, N-EVAP, Organomation Associates, Inc., USA). The lipids were extracted for 6 h according to the same protocol used for the FAME analysis detailed above. The extracted lipid and AXT contents were analyzed by GC and HPLC, respectively. The extraction efficiencies were shown using the total lipid and AXT content percentages, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and optical properties of C-paints
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a shows a schematic illustration of the assembly synthesis of C-paints. Formation of C-paints proceeded via four stages under ultrasound irradiation at a reaction temperature of 60 °C and a reaction time of 2 h under bottom-up synthesis path. Biocompatible polymer PEG as a carbon source is heated (1st step: dehydration) and then converted to carbon-containing precursor (2nd step: carbonization). Consecutively, functionalization (3rd step) followed by passivation. Carbon nanodots are passivated to render fluorescent and hydrophilic surface (4th step: passivation), then finally can obtain liquid-type of carbon nanodot (C-paints). Notably, resulting C-paints in our study contains no acids, bases, surface protectors, or toxic solvents. This study Therefore, it can be expected to have highly efficient structures and properties due to the economics of biocompatible PEGs [39,52]. The C-paints in this study were synthesized via a single process using only PEG without any toxic solvent.

TEM images of the sample were obtained through electron transmission of the C-paints with a high-energy electron beam. The morphology and size distribution of the resulting C-paints were investigated. The average diameter of the C-paints can be estimated by randomly calculating the particle size in the TEM image. As shown in Fig. 1b, the C-paints with sizes ranging from 3 to 5 nm in diameter were evenly distributed with no aggregation. The morphology and size distribution of C-paints were measured using a AFM. The C-paints were evenly distributed across the surface without aggregation (Fig. 1c). In addition, the particle size obtained from topography images measured in multimode were approximately 5 to 7 nm in diameter, which is similar to that measured by TEM image.

It should be noted that the use of larger carbon particles but the same surface passivation agents (average diameter: 30–50 nm) compared with C-paints showed relatively lesser luminescent characteristics [18]. Liu et al. reported that an increase in the reaction temperature from 150 °C to 200 °C decreases the size from 22 to 2 nm and increases quantum yield (QY) [44]. Similarly, surface states and small size are important factors for the emission of CNDs [45]. Furthermore, the PEG passivated C-paints in our study under markedly milder reaction conditions than previous studies (using acid-treated particles at 120 °C for 72 h) will contribute to enhancement of biocompatibility [17].

Zeta potential was determined by (Marvern, Nano ZS90), when the functionalized nanoparticles were dispersed in physiological saline solution followed by pH adjustment using either sodium hydroxide (NaOH, >20%, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) or hydrogen chloride (HCl, >32 wt%, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) solution. (triplicate at 20 °C). Fig. S1 showed Zeta potential of C-paints as a function of pH value. Irrespective of pH, negligible changes of Zeta potential was observed (−0.09 ± 0.35 mV), which is approximately neutral. The result of zeta potential −0.09 ± 0.35 mV were measured.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were used to investigate the bonding composition and functional groups of the synthesized C-paints (Fig. 2a). The C-paints peaks at 1121 cm− 1, 2871 cm− 1, and 3438 cm− 1 were attributed to the –CH2, CH, and OH bonding vibrations, respectively, implying that C-paints possess hydrophilicity, good stability, and water solubility. Based on the FT-IR results, the composition of C-paints passivated with PEG was further confirmed by ultrasound irradiation by high resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2b and Table S1 show the chemical bonding states of the functional carbon and oxygen groups. Noticeable peaks were revealed between 286.0 and 532.0 eV, which could be attributed to C1s and O1s, respectively. In addition, the atomic ratio of carbon to oxygen of C-paints was 2.6, whereas that of PEG is 1.9. This was caused by the crystallization of PEG by the applied energy and circular form of the C-paints. According to the synthesis routines (functionalization and passivation), it is estimated that a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups have been formed by ultrasound irradiation using water without solvent. Which gives the product good water solubility and important for biological applications [46]. Oxygen functional groups can increase the surface properties for photocatalytic and optoelectronic applications on the surface of amorphous carbon nanodots liquid-type C-paints [56].

The resulting C1 peaks for PEG and C-paints were deconvoluted (Fig. S2). C1 peaks of PEG (Fig. S2. PEG (b)) can be divided into three different functional groups, –C–C or –C–H (284.6 eV), –C–O (H) (286.2 eV), and C–O–C=O (287.3 eV) peaks. In contrast, the area of –C–C or –C–H groups for C-paints (Fig. S2. C-paints (b)) almost doubled, by showing 19.1%. In addition, the –COOH (288.6 eV) group newly appeared after passivating with PEG, suggesting a carbonyl-rich surface. Hydrophilic oxygen groups indicate the good spreadability of C-paints in an aqueous solution, suggesting their potential application in culturing of microalgae and biochemical target fields. These changes in C-paints after PEG passivation shown in FT-IR and XPS results indicate that sufficient carbonyl groups were generated and functionalized on the surface of C-paints [47]. These results show that C-paints with potential water-solubility can be prepared by a simple, one-step ultrasound irradiation method without additional chemical modifications.

The fluorescence behavior of C-paints with various excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 550 nm was investigated, as displayed in Fig. 3 . The photoluminescence (PL) peaks were observed in a range from 325 to 425 nm, irrespective of excitation wavelengths. The strongest fluorescence emission was observed at 375 nm when excited at 470 nm. C-paints with a variety of excitation wavelengths have been shown to fluoresce from true UV to visible light (322–800 nm), indicating fluorescence characteristics in actual sunlight. This means that light penetrated and dispersed through C-paints can be effectively transmitted to the inside of the culture medium of microalgae in mass culture.

The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were observed in the FT-IR and XPS spectra after PEG passivation. These functional groups on the surface result from the passivation of the hole-trapping sites on the surface of C-paints, which results in an increase in the fluorescence efficiency [30]. Furthermore, evenly distributed C-paints with uniform size, as shown in the TEM results, also lead to good photoluminescence (PL) properties, which agrees well with results of previous studies [44,45,48–50]. Therefore, it can be suggested that C-paints prepared by the facile, one-step ultrasound irradiation can be used as new types of light delivery materials (or system) and possess great potential in biomedical and environmental applications.

Good biocompatibility is key factor to be considered while utilizing C-paints as fluorescent materials for biological and environmental applications [51,52]. To evaluate the possibility of the C-paints for practical applications, the cytotoxicity of C-paints was investigated. In vitro cell viability of C-paints was analyzed using HepG2 (human liver cell line) and A549 (adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cell line) with 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay [20,53]. HepG2 and A549 cells were exposed to different amounts of C-paints concentrations (0–100 mg mL− 1) for 24, 48, and 72 h, as shown in Fig. 4 . The cell viabilities of HepG2 and A549 cells were 96.9% and 91.1%, respectively, when the C-paints concentration was 10 mg mL− 1 with an exposure time of 72 h. At a C-paints concentration of 10 mg mL− 1, the mean cell viability was >90%. These results are comparable to the results of other reports on carbon-based materials applied to biotechnology. For example, Abu-Ghosh et al. prepared N@CDs that are could enhance the formation of microalgal AXT. However, these N@CDs still suffered from issues such as lack of biocompatibility, photostability, and price competitiveness [38]. In contrast, C-paints prepared in this study by the facile, ultrasound irradiation method provided an average cell viability of >90%, irrespective of the concentration or exposure time. This means that C-paints can ensure biocompatibility and non-cytotoxicity, and consequently, they can be safely applied to environmental and biotechnological fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-paints for cultivation of H. pluvialis
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent C-paints can transmit light in multi-directions, leading to an increase in the photodegradation efficiency [54]. In addition, it was observed that C-paints manufactured by the simple and easy method in this study possessed good stability, strong fluorescence, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility. These results suggest that C-paints are expected to be a unique material for promoting microalgae growth, as evidenced by PL emission spectra. Therefore, growth effects of C-paints on H. pluvialis and Chlorella cultures were investigated. The life cycle of H. pluvialis consists of four types of distinguishable cellular morphologies, macrozooids, microzooids, palmella, and hematocysts. Among these, the macrozooids, microzooids, and palmella stages are usually called the “green vegetative phases”. Hematocysts are referred as the “red nonmotile AXT accumulated encysted phase” [55]. Especially, an attempt to increase cell growth at the green stage can be an important strategy to enhance AXT production [56]. Therefore, C-paints were added from the beginning of cultivation to increase H. pluvialis productivity. As the cell growth and cell density increase, light scattering decreases, but the early stress significantly affects growth efficiency, leading to a positive effect on H. pluvialis growth efficiency. Fig. 5a displays a schematic diagram and actual photograph of H. pluvialis cultivation.

H. pluvialis cells with C-paints concentrations ranging from 0.0 to 10.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v) were incubated for 14 days (Fig. 5). The H. pluvialis growth rate was obtained by measuring OD (680 nm) and DCW (g cell L− 1). Fig. 5b and c show similar growth tendencies under various C-paints concentrations. The growth rates under 5.0 and 10.0 mg mL− 1 C-paints concentrations (by measuring DCW) were 1.8 mg mL− 1 (about 11.7% higher than the control cell content). Growth promotion was observed at appropriate C-paints concentrations (1.0 mg mL− 1) when H. pluvialis was cultured. The DCW (g cell L− 1) was increased to 2.0 ± 0.1 g cell L− 1 (about 20.2% higher than the control cell content). C-paints had a positive effect on the enhancement of the H. pluvialis growth rate by increasing the fluorescence efficiency due to PEG passivation.

Imamoglu et al. studied the effect of stress media CO2 (1.5% (v/v) in air at a rate of 1 L min− 1) with high light intensity of 546 μmol photon m− 2 s− 1 (9.1 times higher than this study (60 μmol photon m− 2 s− 1)) on AXT accumulation from H. pluvialis [57]. Similarly, Kobayashi et al. showed that carotenoid biosynthesis of H. pluvialis is affected by light intensity conditions. However, both studies suggested only the effect on carotenoid pigment accumulation rather than growth rate through DCW [58].

Abu-Ghosh et al. conducted algal reddening stage experiments in clear glass tubes containing only 10 mL of vegetative culture. The cultures were then maintained under high light of 450 μmol photon m− 2 s− 1 supplied by fluorescent lamps. They aimed to use simply synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N@CDs) based on carbonized BSA to boost the accumulation of AXT in microalgae [31]. However, the cultivation was carried out at very small volumes with high light (7.5 times higher than this study) and strong reaction conditions (3.3 and 3.0 times higher reaction temperature and times than this study (195 °C for 6 h)), and high productivity was obtained after addition of N@CD, such as dry cell weight relative to control. Above all, a useful substance called AXT was produced, but biocompatibility analysis were not conducted.

By observing the growth process using microscopic images, we did not notice any growth inhibition caused by C-paints (Fig. 5d). The red intensity was broader than the control at 5.0 and 10.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v) at the brown cell stage (Day 6). No inhibitory factors were observed during the cultivation of H. pluvialis. These results are also supported by positive biocompatibility results from the evaluation of cytotoxicity. According to a recent report on cultivation of microalgae, the cells became extinct in a short culture period with increasing light intensity, and the dead cells were empty (cell dead conditions: 30 μmol m− 2 s− 1; 8 days, 80 μmol m− 2 s− 1; 6 days, and 150 μmol m− 2 s− 1: 5 days) [59]. However, as confirmed by microscopic images (Fig. 5d), dead cells were not observed in this study.

The growth effects of C-paints in green microalga Chlorella sp. KR-1 were also investigated, as shown in Fig. S3. Chlorella sp. KR-1 cells were cultured with various C-paints concentrations (0.0 (no C-paints), 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, and 50.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v)). Cells with C-paints were incubated for 22 days. Interestingly, growth promotion was observed at a C-paints concentration of 5.0 mg mL− 1. The dry cell weight was increased from 1.0 g cell L− 1 to 1.2 ± 0.1 g cell L− 1, which is about 16.4% higher than the content of control cells. In addition, a high C-paints concentration of 50.0 mg mL− 1 led to a slight growth inhibition with a dry cell weight of 0.9 g cell L− 1, which is about 14.4% lower than that of the control sample. As mentioned above, H. pluvialis cell death was induced by increasing light intensity [53]. The stress conditions that promote the productivity of microalgae depend on the microalgae culture conditions and cell growth state. Cells can be adversely affected if they are stimulated above induction conditions. These results show that the appropriate concentration of C-paints with biocompatibility and fluorescence can result in growth promoting effects for the green microalgae, H. pluvialis and Chlorella.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced lipid and AXT induction of C-paints on H. pluvialis
ce:section.ce:para: Bio-TEM images of cell-cross sections were used to compare each subcellular component between the control cells and C-paints treated cells at 10.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v) to confirm AXT accumulation after 14 days of cultivation (Fig. 6 ). To visualize whole cells, the ultrathin serial section method was employed instead of electron tomography because of the large size of Haematococcus cells. The AXT granules are indicated by red arrows, and the C-paints at a concentration of 10.0 mg mL− 1 and control cells were compared for each subcellular component (chloroplast, pyrenoid, oil droplet, starch, and thylakoid) (Fig. 6a). Each component of the cell could be deduced using the H. pluvialis TEM images of previous studies [54]. The overall distribution of AXT granules was much higher in the cells treated with 10.0 mg mL− 1 C-paints in the images showing all organelles (Fig. 6a, cellular organelles row). There was no significant difference in starch content (Fig. 6a, starch row), whereas smaller oil droplets were found to be distributed than in the control, and more abundant forms of AXT granules were observed (Fig. 6a, lipid row). The observed thylakoid appeared to be spiral and united when C-paints were added (Fig. 6a, thylakoid row). Thylakoids degenerate as chloroplast degradation progresses, and as a consequence, chloroplasts exhibit a net-like appearance during the cyst stage. This suggests that the relative volumes of chloroplasts decrease during encystment [8–11]. Studies on ketocarotenoid biosynthesis in Haematococcus also showed that accumulation of AXT occurs outside the chloroplast [60].

To confirm the increase of lipid and AXT production, lipid and AXT contents were investigated after harvest, as depicted in Fig. 7 . H. pluvialis cells treated with C-paints not only showed a considerably fast induction of lipid and AXT contents but also exhibited dramatically raised lipid and AXT extraction efficiency. FAME and AXT (AXT) contents were measured in H. pluvialis cells treated with various C-paints concentrations (0.0, 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v)) after 14 days of cultivation. The lipid-inducing effect of C-paints was the highest at a concentration of 10.0 mg mL− 1 (w/v). The FAME content of cells treated with 10.0 mg mL− 1 of C-paints increased to 1.6 × 10− 6 mg FAME cell− 1 (about 1.3 times higher than the control cell content). In addition, the AXT content was increased to 6.0 × 10− 8 mg AXT cell− 1 (about 1.5 times higher than the control cell content). These results strongly suggested that C-paints played an important role in lipid and AXT accumulation in H. pluvialis (Fig. 7a, b), as shown in the C-paints 10.0 mg mL− 1 column of Fig. 6a. To confirm the improvement of H. pluvialis productivity under non-toxic conditions, experiments were conducted at concentrations of only up to 10 mg mL− 1. As reflected in the cytotoxicity evaluation (Fig. 4), no damage to the cells and organelles was observed. The enhanced pyruvate metabolic pathway provided AXT biosynthesis with primary metabolites as feedstock. The upregulation of genes involved in β-carotene biosynthesis (e.g., PSY, ZDS and lcyB) and β-carotene conversion (e.g., crtZ) accelerated AXT biosynthesis. The upregulation of acetyl-CoA synthetase and acetyl-CoA carboxylase enhanced fatty acid biosynthesis, thus promoting AXT esterification and deposition [61]. C-paints presumably served as light delivery materials when the lipid and AXT accumulation induction of H. pluvialis was mediated through the metabolic pathways described above.

Haematococcus pluvialis biosynthesizes chlorophyll and carotenoid pigment during photosynthesis. Chlorophyll pigments have dual-absorbance ranges, as Chl a absorbs violet-blue (ca. 410–430 nm) and red wavelengths (ca. 660 nm), while Chl b mainly absorbs blue (ca. 450 nm) and orange wavelengths (ca. 640 nm). Another group of accessory pigments, carotenoids, for example β-carotene, astaxanthin, and lutein, are capable of absorbing violet-blue wavelengths (ca. 400–500 nm) which can be outside the range of chlorophyll pigments [62,63]. Carotenoids have also an important role in the dissipation of excess light intensities.
High light delivery effect and PL emission spectra (especially 350 to 500 nm) by the carbon dots might have promote biosynthesis and activities of chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments of H. pluvialis.

Katsuda et al. (2004) reported the enhanced cell growth and astaxanthin accumulation of H. pluvialis using purple (300–460 nm) and blue-purple (340–500) LEDs than normal fluorescent (400–740 nm) lamp (see Table 1 of Katsuda et al., 2004) [64].

C-paints with a variety of excitation wavelengths have been shown to fluoresce from true UV to visible light (322–800 nm), indicating fluorescence characteristics in actual sunlight. This means that light penetrated and dispersed through C-paints can be effectively transmitted to the inside of the culture medium of microalgae in mass culture.

After enhanced growth of biomass, efficient cell disruption and extraction of AXT from H. pluvialis biomass is an important factor [55]. Control cells and cells treated with 10.0 mg mL− 1 C-paints with the highest content of AXT were subjected to solvent extraction using only hexane for recovering FAME and AXT after cultivation. Lipid extraction by hexane was examined to confirm the lipid extraction property of C-paints (50 g L− 1 H. pluvialis loading). The lipid extraction yields of control, 1.0, and 10.0 mg mL− 1 C-paints treated cells were 34.2 ± 2.8%, 53.7 ± 4.1%, and 85.9 ± 0.2%, respectively. (1.0 mg mL− 1: 1.6 times, 10.0 mg mL− 1: 2.5 times higher than the control) (Fig. 7c). Cells cultured with C-paints showed much higher extraction yields than control cells, even without any further pretreatment for extraction. In the case of AXT, the extraction efficiency was also greatly improved. The AXT extraction yields were 46.7 ± 2.6%, 80.1 ± 0.3%, and 84.6 ± 0.4%, respectively (1.0 mg mL− 1: 1.7 times, 10.0 mg mL− 1: 1.8 times higher than control cells) (Fig. 7d).

TEM images (Fig. 6) showed an increase in AXT granules and slight morphological differences of cell organelles. In Fig. 6b-2 and b-4, C-paint treated H. pluvialis cells showed a change in contrast compared with the control cells observed in Fig. 6b-1 and b-3. A gap appeared between the cell wall and cytoplasm. In the case of cell walls, the gap was faint and a little curved (Fig. 6b-2 and b-4). It is estimated that this result is the cause of higher extraction yields as stimulation is applied to the cells of the control. In the cell culture, light and enzyme reactions play an essential role in the production of metabolites. Sun et al. manipulated cell growth by regulating conditions such as pressure and delaying the effects of enzymes on the cell walls [62]. Therefore, appropriate applications may also be possible to alter metabolic pathways for cellular structures such as microalgae cell walls [36]. Depending on various culturing conditions to improve productivity, the morphologies of H. pluvialis cells varied [65].

Typically, after cultivation and harvesting of microalgae, the extraction efficiency is increased through an additional process [66]. Heavy intensity physical and chemical reactions can lead to nutrient losses such as via lipid oxidation. The selection of appropriate cell disruption and extraction methods is largely dependent on the given microalgal species' distinct biology and cell-wall characteristics [67]. The cell walls of H. pluvialis are known to be constituted of an outer trilaminar sheath composed of algaenan (a strong acetolysis-resistant aliphatic biopolymer), a very thick secondary layer below, homogeneously composed mainly of cellulose and mannose, and finally, a tertiary wall heterogeneously composed of cellulose and mannose. Thereby, H. pluvialis extraction is more difficult than extraction from other microalgae [60]. In this study, enhanced extraction efficiency of AXT was obtained without the accumulation and additional extraction processes, which is useful because of stimulation during cultivation. The results of this study proved that the H. pluvialis productivity could be improved by using small carbon particle size, high fluorescence, and water-soluble biocompatible liquid type fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-paints).

C-paints were prepared by ultrasound irradiation at a low reaction temperature of 60 °C for short time of 2 h. A good fluorescence efficiency and water solubility were achieved, which appeared to be influenced by the functionalized surface and uniform particle sizes. In addition, the biocompatibility and non-cytotoxicity of C-paints were evident because a cell viability of >90% was achieved. These excellent properties led to high light delivery effect of non-cytotoxic C-paints and resulted in enhancement of cell growth, induction of lipid and AXT, and extraction efficiency on H. pluvialis. Growth promotion was observed when H. pluvialis was cultured with C-paints. AXT contents 1.5 times higher than those in control cells were obtained in cells treated with 10.0 mg mL− 1 C-paints. AXT contents were 6.0 × 10− 8 mg AXT cell− 1 after 14 days of cultivation. The AXT extraction yield also increased to about 1.8 times higher than control cells (84.6 ± 0.4%). These results show the possibility of C-paints to be applied to the production and microbial metabolism of various species of microalgae and to biopharmaceutical fields including drug delivery and cell imaging.
